skill.name: pinchbench
skill.description: Run PinchBench benchmarks for OpenClaw agents across calendar, email, research, writing, coding, memory, and workflow tasks.
skill.summary: Benchmark OpenClaw agent performance on real-world task suites.
skill.author: pinchbench

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many command, filesystem, network, and environment-access findings. Review confirms most weak-cryptography, root-delete, and documentation command hits are false positives from benchmark text or standard build cleanup. The skill still has high risk because it intentionally runs OpenClaw agents, executes embedded grading Python, clears workspaces, reads secret-bearing environment variables, and uploads benchmark metadata by default.

high_findings.title: Task-Embedded Python Is Executed During Grading
high_findings.description: The grader extracts Python from task files and passes it to exec(). This is arbitrary code execution if tasks are modified, supplied externally, or reviewed less strictly than normal source code.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly calls exec() on extracted grading code and then invokes the resulting grade function. The context confirms this is intentional benchmark behavior, but it is still a strong arbitrary execution primitive.

high_findings.title: Benchmark Prompts Are Sent To Tool-Using Agents
high_findings.description: The runner sends task prompts and session prompts into OpenClaw agents through subprocess calls. These agents operate inside prepared workspaces and may use installed tools, so malicious or unsafe task text can trigger real actions.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess arguments are list-based, which limits shell injection. The risk remains high because untrusted benchmark prompts are deliberately executed by an autonomous agent with workspace access.

high_findings.title: Workspace Reset Can Delete Agent Workspace Contents
high_findings.description: The benchmark resolves an agent workspace and removes it before each task. If the resolved workspace is broader than expected, this can delete user data or OpenClaw workspace state.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The deletion target is derived from OpenClaw configuration or a controlled /tmp fallback. That lowers malicious confidence, but the recursive delete is real and can affect user-controlled workspace paths.

high_findings.title: Benchmark Results Upload External Metadata By Default
high_findings.description: The main runner uploads results unless --no-upload is used. The upload payload includes task scores, task metadata, model identifiers, usage totals, and collected system metadata.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The upload path and payload construction are explicit in source. It appears to support the public leaderboard, not credential theft, but it is default outbound telemetry.

medium_findings.title: Secret-Bearing Environment Variables Are Read
medium_findings.description: The scripts read OPENROUTER_API_KEY, PINCHBENCH_TOKEN, PINCHBENCH_OFFICIAL_KEY, and related settings. This is expected for model validation and uploads, but it increases exposure if combined with network or logging changes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The specific environment variables are visible and are used for authenticated requests. I did not find evidence that the raw secrets are uploaded as payload fields.

medium_findings.title: Docker Build Pipes Remote Installer To Shell
medium_findings.description: The benchmark Dockerfile installs uv through curl piped to sh. This is a common installer pattern, but it executes remote code during image builds without a pinned checksum.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is directly present. It is confined to image build setup, so the risk is supply-chain exposure rather than confirmed malicious runtime behavior.

medium_findings.title: Local Skills Are Copied Into Benchmark Workspaces
medium_findings.description: The runner copies directories from the user OpenClaw skills folder into the benchmark workspace. This can expose local skill content to benchmark agents and later task artifacts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The copy behavior is explicit and intentional. Exposure depends on what local skills contain and what later upload or logging paths include.

medium_findings.title: System Metadata Is Collected For Leaderboard Submission
medium_findings.description: The upload helper gathers platform, release, CPU, memory, and hashed hostname details. This supports benchmark comparison, but it should be disclosed to users before upload.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The metadata keys are directly assembled in code. The hostname is hashed, which reduces sensitivity but does not remove telemetry concerns.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Mostly Text Matches
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography alerts in project metadata, task prose, and asset text appear to match words such as benchmark descriptions, SMART goals, or model names. I found no evidence that these lines implement weak hashing or encryption.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines are descriptive text and metadata, not cryptographic API calls. This makes the blocker classification a false positive for these examples.

low_findings.title: Docker Root Delete Alert Is Package-Cache Cleanup
low_findings.description: The Dockerfile removes /var/lib/apt/lists after apt install. This is standard image cleanup and does not delete root or home directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command target is apt package-list cache under /var/lib, not / or /root. The static critical label is a false positive in this context.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Alerts Are Usage Examples
low_findings.description: Many shell and backtick detections occur inside README and SKILL usage examples. They document benchmark commands rather than executing during skill loading.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are fenced command examples. They are still relevant user instructions, but not hidden execution paths by themselves.

dangerous_patterns.title: exec() On Extracted Task Code
dangerous_patterns.description: Embedded grading code is executed dynamically during automated grading.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct exec() is present on task-derived code and the resulting function is invoked.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent CLI Invocation With Benchmark Prompt Text
dangerous_patterns.description: The runner invokes openclaw agent commands and passes benchmark prompt text as messages.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is built as an argv list, but task prompt text is still handed to a tool-using agent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Workspace Removal
dangerous_patterns.description: The prepared workspace is recursively removed before being recreated for each task.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The recursive delete call is explicit; impact depends on resolved workspace path correctness.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Installer Piped To Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The Dockerfile downloads and executes the uv installer during image build.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The curl-to-shell pattern is direct, but it is a standard build-time installer pattern.

content.value_statement: Agent teams need repeatable evidence before comparing models or publishing leaderboard scores. PinchBench runs structured OpenClaw tasks and records performance across realistic workflows.
actual_capabilities: Runs 23 benchmark tasks covering email, calendar, research, writing, coding, memory, and workflow scenarios.
Creates or reuses OpenClaw agents for a selected model and task suite.
Prepares task workspaces with fixtures before each benchmark run.
Grades tasks with automated checks, LLM judge grading, or hybrid scoring.
Writes benchmark result files with scores, usage data, timing, and task metadata.
Can register and upload benchmark results to the PinchBench leaderboard.
limitations: Requires Python, uv, and a working OpenClaw installation.
Uses command execution, workspace deletion, and network upload paths during normal operation.
Leaderboard upload requires a PinchBench token unless disabled with --no-upload.
Results depend on local tools, installed skills, model access, and network availability.

use_cases.title: Compare Model Performance
use_cases.description: Run the same task suite across models to compare completion quality, cost, and reliability.

use_cases.title: Validate Agent Setup
use_cases.description: Confirm that an OpenClaw agent can handle realistic tasks before using it for production workflows.

use_cases.title: Prepare Leaderboard Submissions
use_cases.description: Generate structured result files and upload qualified runs to the PinchBench leaderboard.

prompt_templates.title: Run A Quick Benchmark
prompt_templates.prompt: Run PinchBench for model <model_id> with the automated-only suite and skip upload.

prompt_templates.title: Run Selected Tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: Run PinchBench for <model_id> on task_01_calendar and task_02_stock. Save results in <output_dir> and do not upload.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Multiple Runs
prompt_templates.prompt: Run PinchBench for <model_id> with <number> runs per task. Summarize mean score, variance, failures, and cost.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare A Publishable Result
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate my PinchBench configuration, run the full suite for <model_id>, review the generated result file, and upload only after I confirm.

output_examples.input: Run the automated-only suite for anthropic/claude-sonnet-4 and skip upload.
output: Benchmark completed for the selected automated tasks.
The result file was saved in the configured results directory.
The summary includes task scores, execution time, token usage, and failed-task notes.

output_examples.input: Compare two PinchBench result files for the same model.
output: The second run improved on calendar and file tasks but regressed on market research.
Average score changed slightly, while execution cost increased due to longer responses.
The most useful next step is rerunning failed hybrid tasks for stability.

output_examples.input: Review my result before leaderboard upload.
output: The run includes all required tasks and model metadata.
Several tasks timed out, so the submission may understate model capability.
Upload should wait until the timeout issue is understood.

best_practices: Run first with --no-upload and inspect generated results before sharing data externally.
Use a dedicated temporary OpenClaw workspace to avoid deleting or exposing unrelated files.
Pin tool versions and review task files before running custom or community task suites.
anti_patterns: Do not run unknown task files because embedded grading code can execute locally.
Do not use a personal workspace that contains private files or unpublished skills.
Do not publish leaderboard results before checking metadata, failures, and upload scope.

faq.question: What does PinchBench measure?
faq.answer: It measures how OpenClaw agents perform on realistic tasks such as email, calendar, research, writing, coding, and workflows.

faq.question: Can I run only part of the benchmark?
faq.answer: Yes. The suite option supports all tasks, automated-only tasks, or a comma-separated list of task IDs.

faq.question: Does it upload results automatically?
faq.answer: The runner uploads by default when configured. Use --no-upload when you want local-only results.

faq.question: Why is this skill high risk?
faq.answer: It executes local commands, clears workspaces, runs embedded grading Python, reads tokens, and can send metadata to a server.

faq.question: Can I add custom tasks?
faq.answer: Yes. Custom task markdown files can define prompts, fixtures, grading criteria, and automated grading code.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The package metadata marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code, with runtime behavior built around OpenClaw.
